coredata.dc:title: Membrane separation assisted colorimetric/fluorescent detection of β-galactosidase-positive bacteria in milk and milk powder based on the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yi
Fu, Li
Su, Huihui
Tang, Li
Wu, Qingxi
Jia, Li
coredata.dc:description: Species-specific enzymes provide a substantial boost to the precision and selectivity of identifying dairy products contaminated with foodborne pathogens, due to their specificity for target organisms. In this study, we developed cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH NSs) for a dual-mode biosensor capable of detecting β-galactosidase (β-Gal)-positive bacteria in milk and milk powder. The sensor exploits the oxidase-mimicking activity of CoOOH NSs, where β-Gal converts the substrate β-D-galactopyranoside to p-aminophenol, reducing CoOOH NSs to Co2+ and inhibiting the formation of the blue product from 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylben-zidine. Sensitivity was enhanced through membrane filtration and β-Gal induction by isopropyl β-D-thiogalactoside. The assay achieved a detection limit of 5 cfu mL−1 and demonstrated recoveries (90.7 % to 103 %) and relative standard deviations <5.7 % in milk and milk powder samples. These findings underscore the potential of the sensor for detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria in dairy products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensor
Bacteria detection
Membrane filtration
CoOOH nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Capture efficiency of bacteria by filter membrane
Investigation of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of β-gal and bacteria
Steady-state kinetic assay of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
Investigation of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
Mechansim of the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel detection of β-Gal-positive bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CoOOH NSs (A). UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH NSs and CoCl2 (Inset: the corresponding photograph) (B). EDS spectra (C), XRD pattern (D), FT-IR spectra (E), and stability (F) of CoOOH NSs. Error bars indicate the standard deviations (n = 3).

UV–vis spectra of the mixture of TMB (0.2 mM) and CoOOH NSs (0.2 mg mL−1) in air- and N2-saturated NaAc-HAc solutions (0.2 M, pH 4.0) for 5 min (A). UV–vis absorption spectra of the catalytic system at various concentrations of IPA (B) and NaN3 (C). Absorbance of RhB at 553 nm exposed to different dosages of CoOOH NSs (D). The concentration of RhB is 5 mM and the concentration of PBQ is 5 mM. n.s. no significant difference (P > 0.05), *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001 (ANOVA). Error bars indicate the standard deviations (n = 3).

UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and fluorescence spectra (B) of blank (CoOOH + TMB), blank + bacteria, blank + PAPG, blank + PAPG + IPTG, blank + PAPG + bacteria, blank + IPTG + bacteria and blank + PAPG + IPTG + bacteria. The concentration of CoOOH NSs is 0.2 mg mL−1 and TMB is 0.2 mM in NaAc-HAc solutions (0.2 M, pH 4.0). The concentration of PAPG is 0.27 mM and IPTG is 1.0 mM. The bacteria employed in the experiment is E. coli O157:H7 (1.0 × 107 cfu mL−1).

UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and the calibration plot of (A 0 - A) versus the logarithm concentration of E. coli O157:H7 (n = 3) (B). Fluorescence spectra (C) and the calibration plot of (F - F 0) versus the logarithm concentration of E. coli O157:H7 (n = 3) (D). The photographs of the detection system (E) and upon irradiation at 254 nm (F) at different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7. The detection conditions are: the concentration of IPTG (1.0 mM), the induction time of IPTG and bacteria (4 h), the pH of the detection system (pH 8.0), the concentration of PAPG (0.27 mM), the incubation time of PAPG, bacteria, and Triton X-100 (2 h), the concentration of Triton X-100 (4 μL mL−1), the dosage of CoOOH NSs (0.2 mg mL−1), the concentration of TMB (0.2 mM), and the reaction temperature (37 °C).

UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding calibration plot (A, B, E, F), and fluorescence spectra and corresponding calibration plot (C, D, G, H) of the sensing system containing different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 in milk and milk powder samples. The detection conditions are the same as in Fig. 4. Error bars indicate the standard deviations (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the principle and the procedure of the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel biosensor for detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria.
Analytical performance of the dual-mode sensor for detecting E. coli O157:H7, generic E. coli, and C. sakazakii.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: y denotes the difference in the absorbance at 652 nm between the absence and presence of bacteria (A 0 − A), and x denotes the logarithm concentration of bacteria.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: y denotes the difference in the fluorescence at λ ex/λ em = 312/420 nm between the absence and presence of bacteria (F − F 0), and x denotes the logarithm concentration of bacteria.

head.ce:title: Membrane separation assisted colorimetric/fluorescent detection of β-galactosidase-positive bacteria in milk and milk powder based on the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Species-specific enzymes provide a substantial boost to the precision and selectivity of identifying dairy products contaminated with foodborne pathogens, due to their specificity for target organisms. In this study, we developed cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH NSs) for a dual-mode biosensor capable of detecting β-galactosidase (β-Gal)-positive bacteria in milk and milk powder. The sensor exploits the oxidase-mimicking activity of CoOOH NSs, where β-Gal converts the substrate β-D-galactopyranoside to p-aminophenol, reducing CoOOH NSs to Co2+ and inhibiting the formation of the blue product from 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylben-zidine. Sensitivity was enhanced through membrane filtration and β-Gal induction by isopropyl β-D-thiogalactoside. The assay achieved a detection limit of 5 cfu mL−1 and demonstrated recoveries (90.7 % to 103 %) and relative standard deviations <5.7 % in milk and milk powder samples. These findings underscore the potential of the sensor for detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria in dairy products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dairy products contaminated with foodborne pathogenic bacteria pose a significant threat to human health and entail considerable economic and social risks (Ortuzar et al., 2018). Among these, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Cronobacter sakazakii (C. sakazakii) are frequently identified in milk and milk powder (Paswan & Park, 2020). The European Union has established a maximum allowable level of 1000 cfu g−1 for E. coli in these samples, and C. sakazakii must be undetectable in a 10 g sample (No 2073/2005). Consequently, the development of sensitive detection methods is imperative for the prevention of foodborne disease outbreaks.

Colorimetric and fluorescent biosensors have garnered interest in the detection of pathogens because of their advantages, including simplicity, speed, and cost-efficiency (Saleh et al., 2024). They do not require costly machinery or specialized instruction, rendering them appropriate for regular examinations in food products, manufacturing plants, and outdoor environments (Sagar Shrikrishna, Sharma, Sahoo, Kaushik, & Gandhi, 2024). Enzymes generated by bacteria exhibit remarkable specificity toward their targets, enhancing the selectivity and precision when identifying particular bacterial strains. Thus, these enzymes, including alkaline phosphatase (Gwynne et al., 2021), tryptophanase (Liu, Yu, Chen and Jia, 2022), and β-galactosidase (β-Gal) (Park et al., 2020) have been employed as biomarkers to construct colorimetric and fluorescent biosensors for identifying enzyme-positive bacteria. Among them, β-Gal is a biomarker for identifying E. coli (Park et al., 2020). However, it has been reported that β-Gal is also present in C. sakazakii (Da Silva Araújo, Esper, Kuaye, Sircili, & Marsaioli, 2012). Nevertheless, there is a lack of reports on using β-Gal for pathogen detection beyond E. coli.

Nanozymes, including Au (Paesa et al., 2023; Téllez-de-Jesús, Flores-Lopez, Cervantes-Chávez, & Hernández-Martínez, 2021) and Ag nanoparticles (Rtimi et al., 2020; Streich et al., 2023), have been employed to construct colorimetric and fluorescent biosensors for detecting β-Gal based on their peroxidase-like activities. However, the use of noble metal nanozymes undoubtedly increases the detection cost. Additionally, these biosensors employ hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to assist peroxidase mimics in catalyzing the oxidation of chromogenic substrates. H2O2 is highly reactive, volatile, and easily decomposed, which makes it inconvenient for storage and transportation (Wu et al., 2019). Thus, the introduction of H2O2 in these biosensors limits their applications. Cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH NSs) are a transition metal-containing nanomaterial with excellent peroxidase-like and oxidase-like activities (Wang et al., 2020). Their oxidase-like activity has been utilized to engineer colorimetric sensors for detecting nitrite (Zhang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023) and acid phosphatase (Wei, Rao, Xue, Luo, & Xue, 2021). It was also used to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescence platform to quantify saxitoxin (Luo et al., 2023). Based on the oxidase-like activities of CoOOH NSs, we anticipate designing a CoOOH NS-based colorimetric/fluorescent sensor for detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria without the need to introduce H2O2.

Nanozyme-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensors are susceptible to interference from complex sample matrices (Arshad, Mohd-Naim, Chandrawati, Cozzolino, & Ahmed, 2022). Separation techniques are an excellent strategy to mitigate this issue (Huang, Li, Feng, Tian, & Duan, 2023). Compared to magnetic separation, membrane filtration is an appealing technique for particle separation and enrichment, as it can concentrate micro-sized bacterial particles onto a two-dimensional plane (Huang et al., 2023). Moreover, it is non-destructive and environmentally friendly, as it avoids the need for harsh chemicals or high temperatures, thus preserving the integrity of the separated bacteria.

This study is dedicated to the development of a CoOOH NS-based dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for the specific detection of β-Gal-positive bacteria in non-fermented milk and milk powder, integrated with a membrane filtration technique. Employing commercially available 0.45 μm cellulose acetate membranes, this method effectively concentrates bacteria while filtering out components smaller than 0.45 μm from the sample matrix. Isopropyl β-D-thiogalactoside (IPTG) was applied to induce β-Gal-positive bacteria, enhancing β-Gal production. β-Gal then converts 4-aminophenyl β-D-galactopyranoside (PAPG) into the reducing agent p-aminophenol (PAP), which reduces CoOOH NSs to Co2+. Unlike Co2+, CoOOH NSs demonstrate enzyme-like catalytic activity, facilitating the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue-purple fluorescent product (oxTMB). TMB emits blue-purple fluorescence at approximately 420 nm when excited at 312 nm (Ngo et al., 2021; Qi, Ding, Lu, & Wang, 2022). Capitalizing on this mechanism, we developed a dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent CoOOH NS-based biosensor for the specific detection of β-Gal-positive bacteria.

β-Gal was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PAPG (98%), PAP (≥98%), TMB (≥99%), 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ, ≥99%), isopropanol (IPA, ≥99.9%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydrate (NaOH, 96%), glacial acetic acid (HAc, ≥99.5%), and sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc, 99%) were achieved from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). IPTG was purchased from BBL Life Science (Shanghai, China). Triton X-100 was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, ≥99%) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The 0.45 μm cellulose acetate membrane and detachable syringe filter were purchased from Haiyan New Oriental Plastic Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiaxing, China). Milk samples were purchased from Yili Milk Industry Co., Ltd. (Inner Mongolia, China). Milk powder samples were sourced from Junlebao Dairy Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) obtained from an ELGA water purification system (ELGA, London, UK) was used. E. coli O157:H7 (GW 1.0202), generic E. coli (ATCC 8739), C. sakazakii (ATCC 29544), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, ATCC 15442), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, CMCC 26003), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium, ATCC 14028), and Shigella sonnei (S. sonnei, CICC 21535) were supplied by Guangdong Microbial Culture Collection Center (Guangzhou, China).

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a micro-spectrophotometer K5600C (Beijing Kaiao Technology Development Company, Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectrophotometer F-380A (Tianjin Gangdong Sci. & Tech. Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China) was used to record the fluorescence spectra.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CoOOH NSs were acquired utilizing a JEM-2100HR bright-field transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A suspension of CoOOH NSs (50 μg mL−1) was first prepared in ultrapure water. Subsequently, the sample was applied dropwise onto a 300-mesh copper mesh, air-dried, and analyzed by TEM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using an iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). The sample was prepared by blending and grinding CoOOH NSs and KBr in a 1:100 (w/w) ratio. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Thermo Fisher Nexsa (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA) over a scanning range of 10 to 90o, with increments of 0.0163o. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Thermo Fisher Nexsa (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was performed on a Bruker ESR A300 spectrometer (Bruker Biospin GmbH, Rheinstetten, Germany) using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a spin-trapping agent.

The Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, C. sakazakii, P. aeruginosa, S. typhimurium, S. sonnei) and Gram-positive bacteria strains (S. aureus) were cultured under aerobic conditions using Luria-Bertani broth (10 mg mL−1 tryptone, 5 mg mL−1 yeast extract, 10 mg mL−1 NaCl, pH 7.0) and the broth (10 mg mL−1 tryptone, 3 mg mL−1 beef extract, 5 mg mL−1 NaCl, pH 7.0) at 37 °C and 200 rpm for a duration of 10 h on a ZHWY-103B shaker (Shanghai Zhicheng Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Afterwards, the bacteria cells were harvested through centrifugation at 10000 rpm (9167g) for 1 min, rinsed twice with phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0), and then reconstituted in the phosphate buffer. The bacterial concentration was calibrated through the assessment of optical density at 600 nm (OD600). The bacterial suspension (100 μL) was inoculated onto Luria-Bertani agar and incubated aerobically at 37 °C, and the resulting concentration was calculated in cfu mL−1. All materials that came into contact with the bacteria were sterilized at 121 °C for 30 min prior to the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capture efficiency of bacteria by filter membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
A 0.45 μm cellulose acetate membrane was first cut into an appropriate shape to install into a detachable syringe filter. Subsequently, the bacterial suspension (1 mL) was injected through the filter membrane. The concentrations of bacteria before and after membrane filtration were measured by the colony assay. The capture efficiency of bacteria (η) was calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
η =
C 0
− C
/
C 0
× 100 %
where C 0 (cfu mL−1) denotes the initial bacteria concentration, and C stands for the bacteria concentration in the filtrate.

CoOOH NSs were synthesized via a redox approach (Wei et al., 2021) with minor modifications. Briefly, 10 mL NaOH solution (1.0 M) was dropped into a 40 mL CoCl2 solution (10 mM) with intense stirring and subjected to sonication for 60 s. Subsequently, 2 mL sodium hypochlorite solution (0.9 M) was introduced, and the blend underwent ultrasonication for 10 min. Centrifugation for 10 min at 8000 rpm (5867g) was employed to gather the sediment. After being rinsed thrice with ultrapure water, the precipitate was dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. Ultimately, the brownish-black CoOOH NSs were placed in a desiccator for storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of CoOOH NSs suspension (100 μL, 0.10–0.22 mg mL−1), 880 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), and 20 μL TMB solution (10 mM) were blended for 5 min. Then, these mixtures were taken for UV–vis measurement at 652 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of β-gal and bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of bacteria suspension (990 μL) were first incubated with IPTG (10 μL, 100 mM) at 37 °C and 200 rpm for 4 h to induce the production of β-Gal. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to filtration through a cellulose acetate membrane with a pore size of 0.45 μm and washed thrice using a 10 mM phosphate buffer at pH 8.0. Subsequently, the membrane containing bacteria cells was transferred to a tube containing 90 μL Triton X-100 (4.5 μL mL−1) and 10 μL PAPG (2.7 mM), and incubated at 37 °C and 200 rpm for 2 h. After that, 20 μL TMB (10 mM), 100 μL CoOOH NSs (0.2 mg mL−1) and NaAc-HAc buffer (780 μL, pH 4.0) were administered to the reaction solutions. The mixture was incubated at ambient temperature in darkness for 5 min. Finally, the mixture was taken for UV–vis absorption and fluorescence measurements. The assay of β-Gal was the same as that of bacteria except for the IPTG induction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
ce:section.ce:para: A steady-state kinetic assay was used to assess the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs. Typically, different concentrations of TMB (20 μL), 880 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), and 100 μL CoOOH NSs suspension were mixed. The influence of TMB concentration on the catalytic performance of CoOOH NSs was investigated by measuring supernatant absorbance at 652 nm at 5 min intervals. The catalytic activity of CoOOH NSs in the presence of E. coli O157:H7 (107 cfu mL−1) using the approach in Section 2.7 “colorimetric and fluorescent detection of β-Gal and bacteria”.

The Lineweaver-Burk plot was employed for the determination of the catalytic kinetic characteristics. The equation representing the Lineweaver-Burk plot formula is described as a mathematical expression (2).
(2)
1 / V = 1 /
V max
+
K m
/
V max
× 1 /
S
The initial velocity V, maximal reaction velocity V max, and Michaelis constant K m are utilized to assess the enzyme's affinity for the substrate, while the TMB concentration is denoted as [S].

Non-fermented milk and milk powder samples were analyzed to evaluate the feasibility of the biosensor in actual samples. The milk sample was diluted 1000-fold before detection to prevent the clogging of the filter membrane by the substances in the sample matrix. The milk powder sample (0.05 g) was thoroughly mixed with 50 mL phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0). Then, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove fats. The milk powder samples were achieved by performing this operation thrice. The spiked specimens were prepared by introducing different concentrations of bacteria to the aforementioned samples and analyzed using the method outlined in Section 2.7 “colorimetric and fluorescent detection of β-Gal and bacteria”.

In triplicate, all experiments were performed, and the findings were displayed as the mean of three replicates ± standard deviation (SD), and statistically evaluated using SPSS software (version 22.0; IBM Corp., Armonk, NY). One-way or muti-way ANOVA was utilized, along with the Bonferroni statistical examination, to establish the significance level, where p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The TEM image of CoOOH NSs (Fig. 1A) reveals a hexagonal lamellar structure with a side length of 65 ± 9 nm, which morphologically suggests an elevated specific surface area and a greater number of catalytic active sites. CoOOH NSs exhibit a brown color and a characteristic peak at 400 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1B) (Zhang, Chen, et al., 2023; Zhang, Huang, et al., 2023). In contrast, the pale pink-colored CoCl2 solution displays a characteristic absorbance at 520 nm (Fig. 1B). The distinct UV–vis spectrophotometry profiles between the CoOOH NSs and the CoCl2 solution confirm the successful preparation of the CoOOH NSs.

The presence of oxygen and cobalt elements in CoOOH NSs was confirmed by the EDS analysis (Fig. 1C), providing evidence of the successful synthesis of CoOOH NSs. Notably, the Cu element originates from the copper mesh used to carry the material. The XRD pattern of CoOOH NSs (Fig. 1D) exhibits three characteristic crystalline peaks at 23.55°, 45.48° and 59.49°, which respectively correspond to the (003), (012), and (018) peaks according to the standard JCPDS card (No. 07–0169) (Lee, Loh, Sow, & Chin, 2012), further confirming the successful preparation of CoOOH NSs. The typical peaks of the functional groups in CoOOH NSs are illustrated in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1E), showcasing the stretching vibrations of −OH (3436 cm−1), Co − O (1624 cm−1), and Co − O2− (588 cm−1) (Luo et al., 2023).

XPS was employed to assess the chemical composition and surface condition of CoOOH NSs. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. S1A) shows no impurities in the CoOOH NSs other than Co, O, and C elements. In the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1B), the prominent peak at 284.59 eV corresponds to the bond of C − C, while the other two peaks represent the bonds of C − O − C (286.20 eV) and O − C=O (287.70 eV). In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1C), there are three kinds of bonds, including Co − OH bond (530.30 eV), oxygen vacancy (531.35 eV), and Co − O bond (529.47 eV) (Chang et al., 2022). The Co 2p spectrum (Fig. S1D) displays two distinct main peaks at 780.22 eV and 795.10 eV, representing Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. The spin-orbit splitting energy between the two Co 2p peaks is 14.88 eV, which agrees with previous literature (Chen, Yang, Yue, & Chen, 2022). The peaks corresponding to Co 2p3/2 are observed at 780.22 eV and 781.80 eV, while the two characteristic peaks of Co 2p1/2 are identified at 795.10 eV and 796.40 eV. The shakeup satellites at 790.10 eV and 805.50 eV indicate the presence of Co(III) in CoOOH NSs, which ensures the oxidation ability of CoOOH NSs. Additionally, Fig. 1F displays that the absorbance of CoOOH NSs at 400 nm decreases <4% after storing them in a desiccator for 7 weeks, demonstrating the stability of the material. The absorbance at 652 nm of the reaction product (oxTMB) of TMB and the stored CoOOH NSs for 7 weeks is similar to that of TMB and the freshly prepared CoOOH NSs (Fig. S2), demonstrating that the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs after storage for 7 weeks remain stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs was explored utilizing TMB as the substrate. Incubation of CoOOH NSs with TMB at room temperature in the dark for 5 min resulted in a mixture that exhibited a blue hue (inset of Fig. S3A) and a distinct absorbance peak at 652 nm, indicative of the oxidase-like characteristics of CoOOH NSs. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm, excited at 312 nm, was found to be diminished in the CoOOH NSs-TMB mixture compared to the TMB solution alone (Fig. S3B). This reduction in fluorescence is attributed to the oxidation of TMB by CoOOH NSs, thereby confirming their oxidase-like activity.

The mechanism of the catalytic oxidation of TMB by CoOOH NSs was studied. First, the impact of O2 on the catalytic system was investigated. Fig. 2A displays that the air-saturated solution displays a more significant absorbance at 652 nm than the N2-saturated solution, suggesting the crucial role of O2 in the catalytic oxidation of TMB by CoOOH NSs.

Second, the presence and nature of reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced within the catalytic system were analyzed. IPA, NaN3, and PBQ are effective scavengers of hydroxyl radical (•OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide anion (O2 •−), respectively (Kailasa, Vajubhai, Koduru, & Park, 2023; Shi et al., 2017). The absorbance at 652 nm remained unaffected by varying concentrations of IPA (Fig. 2B), indicating the absence of •OH in the system. Conversely, an increase in NaN3 concentration led to a decrease in absorbance (Fig. 2C), suggesting the generation of 1O2, which was corroborated by ESR spectroscopy using the spin-trapping agent TEMP, revealing characteristic peaks of TEMP-1O2 adducts in the ESR spectrum of CoOOH NSs (Fig. S4). While PBQ is a common O2 •− scavenger, its reactivity with TMB necessitated the use of rhodamine B (RhB) to assess O2 •− presence. The degradation of RhB by O2 •− results in a reduced absorbance at 553 nm (Popa et al., 2020). Introducing CoOOH NSs causes a decrease in the absorbance of RhB at 553 nm (Fig. 2D), demonstrating the presence of O2 •−. Contrastingly, PBQ inhibits the alteration of the absorbance at 553 nm, confirming that the produced O2•− is removed by PBQ (Deng, Tu, Fu, Liu, & Jia, 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechansim of the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel detection of β-Gal-positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 depicts the process and principle of the dual-mode biosensor for the detection of β-Gal-positive bacteria. The assay comprises four steps: (1) Inducing the production of large amounts of β-Gal by IPTG; (2) Enriching bacteria cells using membrane filtration; (3) Releasing β-Gal via lysing bacterial cell membranes using Triton X-100; (4) β-Gal can react with its substrate PAPG to form a strongly reducing substance (PAP). PAP can reduce CoOOH NSs to generate Co2+. CoOOH NSs possess oxidase-like activity and oxidize the TMB substrate to produce blue oxTMB. In contrast, Co2+ does not possess oxidase-like activity. Thus, the presence of β-Gal-positive bacteria decreased the formation of oxTMB in the system owing to the reduction of CoOOH NSs by PAP. TMB exhibits fluorescence emission at approximately 420 nm when excited at 312 nm. Consequently, a CoOOH NS-based colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode biosensor was constructed to detect β-Gal-positive bacteria.

E. coli O157:H7 was used as a model of β-Gal-positive bacteria to verify the mechanism of the biosensor. Fig. 3A shows that the absorbance of the mixture of CoOOH NSs and TMB at 652 nm remains unchanged in the absence of bacteria (curves a, c, and d). Without PAPG in the system, the presence of bacteria almost does not cause the change of the absorbance at 652 nm (curves b and f). The addition of IPTG significantly reduces the absorbance of the system at 652 nm (curve g), compared to the absence of IPTG (curve e). The same results were achieved by observing the change in the fluorescence of the system at λ ex/λ em = 312/420 nm, as shown in Fig. 3B.

A steady-state kinetic assay was used to evaluate the influence of bacteria on the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs. According to the Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. S5), the K m values of CoOOH NSs in the absence and presence of E. coli were calculated to be 0.066 mM and 0.11 mM, respectively. The higher K m of CoOOH NSs in the presence of E. coli suggests that E. coli reduces the binding affinity of CoOOH NSs for TMB. Furthermore, the V max of CoOOH NSs in the presence of E. coli (9.75 × 10−6 M s−1) is lower than that in the absence of E. coli (11.6 × 10−6 M s−1), demonstrating E. coli decreases the catalytic activity of CoOOH NSs. These results indirectly demonstrate that in the presence of E. coli, CoOOH NSs are damaged, resulting in a decline in their oxidase-mimicking activity.

The capture efficiency of membrane filtration for bacteria was assessed. As shown in Fig. S6, the membrane filtration method can capture over 97% of bacteria, verifying the effectiveness of the technique. It was compared with other separation approaches, including membrane filtration and magnetic particle-based methods, as listed in Table S1. The membrane filtration demonstrates superior capture efficiency compared to these other approaches. However, the pores of the filter membrane can easily become blocked by substances in the sample matrix, which may result in a decline in filtration performance.

The decrease in the absorbance at 652 nm (A 0 - A) in the presence of bacteria (A) compared to the absence of E. coli O157:H7 (A 0) was used as an indicator to optimize the detection conditions. A larger (A 0 - A) was used as the optimization goal since it suggests that the presence of bacteria can induce a more significant absorbance response. Figs. S7A − H demonstrate the optimal detection performance under the following conditions: the concentration of IPTG (1.0 mM), the induction time of IPTG and bacteria (4 h), the pH of the detection system (pH 8.0), the concentration of PAPG (0.27 mM), the incubation time of PAPG, bacteria, and Triton X-100 (2 h), the concentration of Triton X-100 (4.0 μL mL−1), the dosage of CoOOH NSs (0.2 mg mL−1), and the concentration of TMB (0.2 mM).

The dual-mode sensor was used to analyze β-Gal-positive bacteria commonly existed in milk and milk powder, including E. coli O157:H7 (Fig. 4 ), generic E. coli (Fig. S8), and C. sakazakii (Fig. S9). Figs. 4A−4D, S8, and S9 depict that the decreased absorbance at 652 nm and the increased fluorescence at λ ex/λ em = 312/420 nm linearly responds to the corresponding logarithm bacteria concentration ranging from 101 to 107 cfu mL−1. The color of the solutions turns lighter shade of blue with the increased bacteria concentration (Fig. 4E). In contrast, increasing bacteria concentration strengthens the blue-purple fluorescence emitted by the solutions upon irradiation at 254 nm (Fig. 4F). Table 1 lists the calibration function and the LOD of each bacterium. The determination coefficients (R 2) of the linear equations are higher than 0.952. The LOD of the colorimetry channel for each bacterium is 5 cfu mL−1 according to 3-fold signal-to-noise ratio. By contrast, the LOD of the fluorimetry for each bacterium is <7 cfu mL−1.

Additionally, the impact of IPTG induction on detection sensitivity was evaluated by comparing the LOD with and without induction. As depicted in Figs. S10A and S10B, without IPTG induction, the sensor can detect down to 103 cfu mL−1, which is significantly higher than the LOD value of 5 cfu mL−1 achieved after induction. This comparison underscores the significance of IPTG induction in enhancing sensitivity.

P. aeruginosa and S. aureus not generating β-Gal were selected as the negative controls to assess the specificity of the dual-mode biosensor. Additionally, S. typhimuriumn was chosen due to its similar morphological and physiological characteristics to E. coli and C. sakazakii (Sinsinbar et al., 2020). S. sonnei possesses similar physiological properties to E. coli (Torraca, Holt, & Mostowy, 2020; Tsai et al., 2022). Figs. S11A and 11B illustrate that P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and S. typhimuriumn (107 cfu mL−1 for each bacterium) do not cause a decreased absorbance at 652 nm and an increased fluorescence at λ ex/λ em = 312/420 nm. In contrast, β-Gal-positive bacteria (E. coli, C. sakazakii, and S. sonnei) trigger significant A 0 - A and F - F 0 values. The results suggest that the biosensor exhibits remarkable specificity for β-Gal-positive bacteria due to the exceptional specificity of β-Gal.

The contemporary dairy products market is divided into two principal categories: fermented and non-fermented dairy products (Ranadheera, Vidanarachchi, Rocha, Cruz, & Ajlouni, 2017). β-gal-producing probiotics, such as Lactobacillus spp., Bifidobacterium spp., and Streptococcus thermophilus, are predominantly found in raw and unpasteurized milk. They are not usually present in pasteurized, finished milk, or spray-dried milk powder due to the effectiveness of pasteurization and spray drying in eliminating the majority of bacteria (O'Sullivan, Norwood, O'Mahony, & Kelly, 2019). In contrast, fermented dairy products like yogurt and yogurt drinks intentionally incorporate these probiotics to promote fermentation and enhance probiotic content. Most probiotics are relatively temperature-sensitive and typically lose their activity at temperatures between 60 and 75 °C (Hao et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021). However, the spores of certain heat-resistant pathogenic bacteria may still survive following high-temperature treatments (Radmehr, Zaferanloo, Tran, Beale, & Palombo, 2020). To mitigate the influence of β-Gal-producing probiotics, samples of non-fermented milk and milk powder were specifically tested.

The plate-counting method did not detect E. coli and C. sakazakii in the blank samples. Therefore, the spiked sample samples were analyzed. Figs. 5 , S12 − S15 demonstrate that the (A 0 - A) and (F - F 0) values linearly respond to the logarithmic concentration of each bacterium in milk and milk powder samples, with R 2 higher than 0.985. Experiments to assess the sensor's accuracy were carried out at various concentrations (103, 104, 105, and 106 cfu mL−1), demonstrating its reliability. Table S2 demonstrates that the recoveries range from 90.7% to 103%, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) <5.7%, indicating that the sensor can accurately detect β-Gal-positive bacteria in non-fermented milk and milk powder.

The sensor was compared with other detection approaches for E. coli and C. sakazakii, as detailed in Tables S3 and S4. It demonstrates superior LOD and a broader linear range, highlighting the superiority of the sensor in detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria.

A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-channel biosensor was developed for detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria in non-fermented milk and milk powder based on the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NSs. The sensor used membrane filtration to enrich bacteria and remove other sample matrices. IPTG was used to induce bacteria to produce large amounts of β-Gal. These two schemes can significantly improve the detection sensitivity of β-Gal-positive bacteria. The sensor can detect as low as 5 cfu mL−1 E. coli and C. sakazakii. Furthermore, it exhibits excellent selectivity toward β-Gal-positive bacteria owing to the high specificity of β-Gal. The feasibility of the sensor was confirmed by successfully detecting milk and milk powder, with recoveries ranging from 90.7% to 103% and RSDs <5.7%. The results demonstrate that the sensor will have significant potential in detecting β-Gal-positive bacteria in milk and milk powder. Nevertheless, the LOD of C. sakazakii needs to comply with the standards set by the European Union. To achieve this, the detection method should be further improved to eliminate the need for an elution step, thereby improving the detection sensitivity of C. sakazakii (e.g., fluorescence probe-based sensor directly integrated onto the filtering membrane).

Yi Fan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Fu: Validation. Huihui Su: Validation. Li Tang: Validation. Qingxi Wu: Validation. Li Jia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675056).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, G., Huang, Z., Hu, L. W., Wang, Y. M., Deng, S. L., … Lai, W. H. (2023). Molecular engineering powered dual-readout point-of-care testing for sensitive detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7. ACS Nano, 17(23), 23723−23731. doi:10.1021/acsnano.3c07509

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The target-induced redox and diazotized reaction for colorimetric ratio detection of nitrite using CoOOH nanosheets as mimetic oxidase
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, J. N., Chen, H. Y., Liu, J., Gui, J. L., Liu, M. L., … Yao, S. Z. (2023). The target-induced redox and diazotized reaction for colorimetric ratio detection of nitrite using CoOOH nanosheets as mimetic oxidase. Talanta, 258, 124458. doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124458
